12B Haverstock Close, London, E14 2PQ 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He describes recurring episodes since adolescence with preserved awareness in some events and collapse with shaking in others, but with variable semiology and no consistent focal features reported by witnesses. He has a remote history of mild traumatic brain injury in his early 20s without documented loss of consciousness beyond a few minutes and no neurosurgical intervention. 

Since his last community review six months ago, he reports clusters of brief warning sensations (described as a rising internal feeling and metallic taste) on some days, and a smaller number of longer events with loss of awareness. He estimates the more disruptive events occur q2 - 3wk, often later in the evening, sometimes after poor sleep. There is no consistent relation to alcohol intake, and he does not drive. Post-event confusion lasts 10–20 minutes, with fatigue into the following day. There have been no tongue bites or significant injuries in the last year, though he notes occasional myalgia afterwards. Family videos are inconclusive due to partial view and low light. 

Medication at present: Levetiracetam 1000 mg b.d. and Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. He has tolerated these with intermittent irritability (patient-reported) and mild sleep fragmentation; no rash, no gastrointestinal upset. Adherence is reported as good, supported by pharmacy refill records. He uses a phone reminder system for dosing. There is no history of status episodes or emergency department presentations in the past 12 months. 

Examination today was unremarkable with no lateralising neurological signs. Routine bloods last month (FBC, U&E, LFTs, TFTs, vitamin D) were within reference limits. An MRI brain (3T epilepsy protocol) performed in 2023 was reported as structurally normal without mesial temporal sclerosis, focal cortical dysplasia, or microhaemorrhage. Interictal EEG in 2022 showed rare non-specific slowing without epileptiform discharges. 

Plan: Given the reported frequency of more disruptive events at approximately q2 - 3wk and the historical variability in semiology, I have arranged ambulatory EEG with home video capture to improve event-characterisation. I have also requested retrieval of prior imaging for re-review at the neuroradiology meeting in case of subtle sequelae of the remote mild traumatic brain injury. For now, I have not altered medication pending these results. We discussed sleep hygiene, stress management strategies (patient-led), and the value of witness recordings when safe. He is aware of safety precautions regarding bathing, cooking with open flames, and working at heights. 

I will see him again in three months or sooner if the ambulatory EEG captures typical events earlier. He knows to contact the service if frequency increases beyond the current q2 - 3wk pattern or if new features emerge such as prolonged confusion, focal neurological deficits, or injury. 

Yours sincerely

Focal Epilepsy

Transition to Adult Services: This was Daniel Hughes’s first review in the adult neurology clinic following transition from paediatric services. His mother attended and provided collateral history with his consent. We discussed ongoing management, self-management skills, and plans for shared decision-making as he settles into adult care.

Epilepsy Type: The history is consistent with focal epilepsy, with focal impaired awareness episodes characterised by brief behavioural arrest, vacant expression, and subtle right-hand automatisms lasting 15–30 seconds, followed by mild post-event confusion.

Seizures: His absence seizures are now occurring on three days of the week, whereas previously they were restricted to once every couple of weeks. He and his mother report that events cluster towards late afternoon on school days, with occasional brief myoclonic jerks on awakening but no generalised convulsions. There is no tongue biting, injury or incontinence reported.

Medication: Currently on Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily since February 2025 (converted from Oxcarbazepine due to mood-related adverse effects). He uses Buccal Midazolam 5 mg as a rescue plan only if any event exceeds 3 minutes (not required to date). No other regular medications.

Adherence and Tolerability: He reports good adherence using a phone reminder app. Mild irritability noted in the first month of Levetiracetam initiation has settled; no current mood or sleep concerns.

Triggers and Lifestyle: He notes poorer sleep during exam periods and skipped lunches on busy college days. No alcohol or recreational drug use reported. He is starting driving lessons planning for the future; we reviewed DVLA guidance regarding seizure freedom requirements.

Investigations and Prior Results: Childhood MRI (age 12) reported as normal. Paediatric EEG (age 15) showed left temporal interictal spikes. No adult EEG completed since transition; we discussed updating EEG to reassess current interictal activity. Bloods (July 2025) normal FBC/U&Es/LFTs; Vitamin D borderline low (48 nmol/L) and he is taking over-the-counter supplementation per GP advice.

Safety and Support: Seizure first-aid and risk minimisation reviewed, including showering rather than bathing unsupervised, and kitchen safety precautions. His mother remains an emergency contact and will continue to attend when possible to provide collateral information.

Plan Agreed Today:
- Continue Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily without change pending updated assessment.
- Arrange routine EEG and consider ambulatory EEG if interictal yield is low but clinical suspicion remains high.
- Maintain seizure diary capturing time of day, precipitating factors, and recovery profile.
- Optimise sleep hygiene and regular meals; college letter offered to support adjustments during assessments.
- DVLA advice reiterated; not currently driving. Will revisit once seizure control improves and statutory requirements are met.
- Telephone review in 8 weeks to reassess frequency and discuss results, with in-person follow-up in 4–6 months or sooner if deterioration occurs.

Yours sincerely

Thank you for asking me to review this patient in our epilepsy clinic. He attended with his partner, who provided collateral history. He works in a cleanroom/data-hall environment with strict access controls. He and his partner report that retrieval of rescue medication and obtaining refills is often significantly delayed while on site, which has led to more frequent missed doses. They both feel his seizure control has noticeably worsened over recent months in this context.

He has a history consistent with focal epilepsy. The typical events begin with a brief epigastric rising sensation and déjà vu, followed by behavioural arrest with rightward head version and non-purposeful picking movements of the left hand, lasting 60–90 seconds, with post-ictal confusion for up to 20 minutes. Secondary generalisation is rare but has occurred twice this year, both during early shifts when access to his medication locker was delayed. Weekly morning clusters reported; number per cluster not documented. Clusters are most often reported on days when doses are delayed at the start of his shift.

He is currently prescribed lamotrigine and lacosamide. He admits to missed or delayed morning doses on high-security access days, and to difficulty accessing buccal midazolam when a cluster begins. There is no recent head injury, intercurrent infection, or sleep deprivation beyond early shift starts. Alcohol intake is minimal. No new focal neurological symptoms between events.

Examination today was unremarkable. Routine bloods last month were within reference ranges. A prior MRI (2019) reported left mesial temporal sclerosis; interictal EEG (2020) showed left temporal sharp waves. Taken together with his semiology, this remains consistent with focal epilepsy of left temporal onset.

In summary, he has focal epilepsy with deterioration in seizure control strongly associated with delayed access to both regular and rescue medication in his workplace. Weekly morning clusters reported; number per cluster not documented. This pattern represents a clear decline from his prior baseline.

Plan:
- Optimise adherence: we discussed strategies to avoid delayed dosing at shift start, including pre-shift dosing and carrying a day-dose in a tamper-evident, security-approved container, as per employer policy (patient to confirm feasibility with occupational health).
- Rescue plan: reinforced use of buccal midazolam at onset of a second event within a cluster as previously instructed; patient to explore on-person carriage within workplace rules.
- Safety: advised temporary suspension of lone working and work at heights pending improved control (patient to liaise with line manager and occupational health).
- Investigations: arrange updated EEG and drug levels to exclude subtherapeutic dosing; consider ambulatory EEG if events remain frequent.
- Treatment: if clusters persist despite adherence measures, we will consider adding brivaracetam or perampanel; patient agrees in principle.
- Follow-up: 6 weeks, sooner if further generalised seizures or injuries occur.

Thank you for your ongoing care. Please let me know if there are workplace policy documents we should review jointly to facilitate safer access to his medications on site.

Yours sincerely,

Thank you for asking me to review your patient regarding recurrent events concerning for seizures. His type 2 diabetes is managed in primary care and was not the focus of today’s consultation; he reports stable glycaemic control and no recent hypoglycaemic episodes. Our discussion centred on the paroxysmal events.

He describes brief lapses in awareness with a sudden pause in activity and a blank stare, occasionally accompanied by a slight head nod. There is no clear warning. He typically resumes conversation within seconds but can feel mentally “foggy” for several minutes afterwards. His partner has witnessed some of these and confirms there is no tonic–clonic activity, tongue biting, or incontinence. He has not had injuries.

Regarding timing and burden, he does not keep a diary and finds it hard to quantify overall frequency. When clusters occur, typically two - three absences over ~1 h; frequency not tracked. He has noticed that poor sleep and missed meals may precede episodes, though he is unsure if this is consistent. There is no clear diurnal pattern and no specific photic triggers identified. He does not drive.

Past imaging and EEG data are limited. He recalls a routine EEG several years ago reported as non-diagnostic; we do not yet have the tracings or formal report. He has not had prolonged monitoring. There is no history of febrile convulsions, CNS infection, or significant head injury. Family history is non-contributory. He is not on anti-seizure medication currently and prefers to avoid treatment until we have a clearer characterisation of the events. He drinks alcohol rarely and denies recreational drug use.

On examination today, general and neurological examinations were unremarkable. Cardiovascular observations were normal and there were no orthostatic symptoms. Capillary glucose in clinic was within his usual range by his report (home reading prior to attendance). Mood and cognition appeared appropriate; no features suggestive of an acute mental health issue were evident.

Plan discussed with the patient:
- Arrange routine EEG with hyperventilation and photic stimulation, and request access to the previous EEG report if available.
- Book ambulatory EEG or home video-EEG monitoring to increase the chance of capturing typical events.
- MRI brain with epilepsy protocol to evaluate for a structural substrate.
- Provide seizure first aid advice and recommend a simple event diary to document date/time, context, potential triggers, and any witness observations.
- Liaise with primary care regarding continued diabetes management and to ensure hypoglycaemia is minimised as a potential confounder.

We will review him after investigations to discuss findings and next steps. He is aware to seek urgent care for prolonged events, repeated clusters not returning to baseline, or new features such as injury or confusion lasting longer than usual.

Yours sincerely,

He states that recognising this pattern allows him to pause, sit if needed, and perform slow breathing until the sensation subsides. He has not reported loss of awareness or generalised convulsions.

Seizures: He reports a myoclonic jerk every other week, typically in the early morning within an hour of waking. These brief single twitches have not led to falls or injuries. There have been no emergency presentations, and no episodes of tongue biting, incontinence, or post-ictal confusion. Overall, he feels the pattern is stable and manageable with his coping strategies.

Focal epilepsy type: Based on the semiology and consistent focal auras with preserved awareness, the working diagnosis remains focal epilepsy, likely temporal onset. He is keen to continue with self-monitoring and aura management techniques.

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Motor and coordination normal. Gait normal. No focal neurological deficits. Weight and blood pressure within target range.

Plan:
- Continue levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily; no dose change today as events are brief and non-injurious.
- Reinforce aura recognition and safety measures (sit down, controlled breathing, avoid hazardous activity during warnings).
- Sleep hygiene and stress reduction discussed; patient will continue diary documenting timing, context, and any triggers around auras and myoclonic events.

Cc: Dr Michael O’Connor (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Priya Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Clinical deterioration and treatment review

Hi Emily

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Focal epilepsy. The patient has a history consistent with focal aware and focal to bilateral tonic–clonic seizures.

Present Medication:

1. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
2. Carbamazepine MR 400 mg twice daily

Recent Clinical Course and Present Seizure Frequency:

The patient had been seizure-free since 29/09/2017. Following a recent viral illness two weeks ago, there has been a clear deterioration with breakthrough events despite adherence and no identifiable lifestyle triggers. The patient reports about three - ten seizures in the last two weeks, described as brief focal aware episodes with déjà vu and epigastric rising sensations, some progressing to impaired awareness; two events were witnessed by family with right arm posturing and head deviation to the right. No injuries reported, but marked post-ictal fatigue and reduced confidence in daily activities.

Context and Triggers:

- Viral illness 14 days ago with fever, myalgia, and poor sleep for 3–4 nights.
- No missed doses reported; pharmacy refill history corroborates adherence.
- No alcohol intake; maintains regular meals and hydration; no new medications other than paracetamol during the illness.

Plan of Action:

Given the deterioration after a prolonged remission, we will optimise antiseizure therapy and arrange urgent investigations:

- Increase Lamotrigine to 175 mg twice daily for 1 week, then 200 mg twice daily thereafter, provided no rash or adverse effects develop.
- Continue Carbamazepine MR 400 mg twice daily for now; we will review levels and consider reduction or switch if adverse effects or interactions are suspected.
- Bloods this week: full blood count, U&E, LFTs, CRP, lamotrigine and carbamazepine trough levels.
- EEG with sleep deprivation requested; repeat MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) as last imaging was over five years ago.
- Provide safety advice: avoid heights, open water, and unsupervised bathing; advise no driving and to inform DVLA due to recurrence of seizures.
- If seizure clusters persist (more than two focal impaired awareness events in 24 hours), patient to attend ED for assessment and consideration of rescue therapy.

Follow-up:

I have arranged an urgent review in clinic in 4–6 weeks. We will reassess frequency, tolerability, and results. If seizures continue despite the above, we will consider adding lacosamide or levetiracetam as adjunctive therapy and discuss referral to the surgical work-up pathway if focal onset is further localised.

With best wishes

Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 14 June 2015 

Dr Amelia Patel  
St George’s Hospital  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Amelia Patel,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 22-03-1988, Hospital No: H473920 NHS No. 9938475612  
18 Willow Lane, Wimbledon, London SW19 2PA 

Diagnoses:  

Two unprovoked seizures under evaluation; EEG pending (booked July 2015).  

Current Medication:  

No regular antiepileptic medication at present. PRN buccal midazolam not prescribed.  

Plan:  

- Arrange routine EEG and brain MRI with epilepsy protocol.  
- Provide written seizure first-aid and SUDEP risk counselling.  
- Discuss driving regulations and notify DVLA as appropriate.  
- Review in clinic after investigations; earlier if further events occur.  
- Liaise with community pharmacy regarding continuity of supply if treatment is initiated.  

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today following two recent events suggestive of seizures. The first seizure was reported in April 2015 while visiting relatives in Canada. It happened during an afternoon nap, with an unusual tingling spreading from the right hand to the shoulder. The second event took place in June 2015 in the USA, again from sleep, lasting approximately five minutes, with comparable symptoms. There was no clear precipitant identified. There is no personal history of significant head injury, CNS infection, or febrile convulsions. Family history is non-contributory by the patient’s account. Alcohol intake is modest and there is no recreational drug use reported. Sleep had been irregular around the times of travel.  

Past variability in pharmacy supply is documented in the records from previous long-term prescriptions for non-neurological conditions; the patient reports that adherence was impacted at times when pharmacies substituted brands or experienced stock interruptions. We discussed the importance of consistency of supply should an antiseizure medicine be commenced, and I will ensure any future prescription specifies brand consistency and that the community pharmacy is briefed to minimise switching.  

On examination today, general and neurological examinations were normal. Observations were stable. There are no lateralising signs.  

Given the limited number of events, absence of ongoing neurological deficit, and pending investigations, we agreed not to start an antiseizure medication today. Should there be any recurrence, especially with evolving features, we will reconsider treatment promptly. The patient has been advised on sleep hygiene, avoidance of sleep deprivation, and to keep a clear record of any further episodes including timing, duration, semiology, and potential triggers.  

I have advised that they must not drive and should follow DVLA guidance for a first unprovoked seizure, with notification to the DVLA; I have provided supporting documentation. Workplace safety and water safety precautions were reviewed. A seizure action plan and emergency guidance have been supplied.  

I will see Michael Turner after the EEG and MRI are completed, or sooner if further events occur. Please let me know if there are questions or if additional information from primary care becomes available.  

With best wishes,

Subject: Treatment review and titration plan

Dear Dr Patel,

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient reports both generalised and focal epilepsy).

Background and context:

He cycles to work most days and reports that his routine is relatively consistent; this has been recorded for context. No recent intercurrent illness or sleep deprivation was volunteered.

Current Medication:

1. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
2. Brivaracetam 50 mg twice daily

Previous Anti-Seizure Medications (patient-reported):

- Topiramate (stopped due to cognitive slowing)
- Sodium valproate (stopped when planning family; weight gain reported)

Present Seizure Frequency:

Morning clusters one - two×/month; ~four events over 90 min. He describes these as brief focal aware episodes with rising epigastric sensation and speech hesitation, occasionally progressing to generalised convulsions if the cluster is prolonged. There have been no injuries reported during recent events.

Assessment and Plan of Action:

The pattern suggests persistent focal onset seizures with secondary generalisation within the context of combined generalised and focal epilepsy. Given partial response to lamotrigine and brivaracetam, I propose introducing lacosamide as adjunctive therapy and then reassessing. If tolerated, we will consider rationalising the regimen at follow-up.

Lacosamide titration schedule:
- Week 1: 50 mg at night
- Week 2: 50 mg twice daily
- Week 3: 50 mg in the morning and 100 mg at night
- Week 4: 100 mg twice daily (continue at this dose thereafter pending review)

I have arranged a follow-up appointment in four months. At that visit, if stability is maintained and lacosamide is well tolerated, we will consider gradual reduction of brivaracetam while maintaining lamotrigine.

With best wishes,

I reviewed Emily Carter in clinic today. There is no clear precipitant beyond sleep disruption and travel-related stress, and there are no consistent focal warnings reported.

She also had a brief trial of Topiramate (up to 50 mg twice daily) which was stopped for cognitive slowing. She feels Levetiracetam has been better tolerated overall, with occasional irritability that she manages through exercise and structured routines. 

We discussed strategies to mitigate travel-related triggers including sleep prioritisation, hydration, avoiding alcohol, and using aisle seats to reduce stress. She expresses interest in graded exposure and psychological support to address her travel anxiety, which I believe is appropriate.

Summary: Ongoing seizures with variable semiology; prior non-diagnostic EEGs; MRI brain (2006) reported no clear structural correlate. Lifestyle factors include prolonged screen exposure and irregular sleep due to leading a university esports club.

Current context: I saw Wendy Brown in clinic today; she attended alone. She reports a recent shift in her seizure pattern over the past three months, characterised by clusters arising after nights of curtailed sleep, most often grouped during the latter half of the night with occasional continuation into the early morning period before waking fully. She links these episodes to late-night tournaments and extended screen time. There have been no reported convulsions resulting in injury, but she describes brief impaired awareness episodes and occasional lip-smacking witnessed by teammates on voice chat; recovery is usually within minutes, followed by marked fatigue.

Prior investigations: Multiple routine EEGs previously unremarkable or non-specific. An MRI from 2006 reportedly showed no lesion to explain seizures. No recent cardiac concerns; past echocardiogram (2008) was within normal limits by report.

Medication history: Trials in the past have included carbamazepine, valproate, phenytoin, phenobarbital, gabapentin, levetiracetam (reduced severity but increased fatigue and irritability), clobazam (both intermittent and regular), vigabatrin, and lacosamide. She has not previously taken topiramate, zonisamide, pregabalin, tiagabine, acetazolamide, primidone, perampanel, retigabine, or rufinamide. She currently uses metoclopramide as needed for nausea and takes folic acid 5 mg daily alongside a general multivitamin.

Interim history: Over the summer she increased her coaching commitments, often streaming until 02:00–03:00. She notes more episodes on nights preceded by long scrimmage reviews with multiple screens and blue-light exposure. She has attempted blue-light filters and short naps, with limited benefit. She denies new focal neurological symptoms, significant weight change, or mood disturbance beyond tiredness and reduced motivation following clustered events.

Examination today: General and neurological examinations were unremarkable. No cognitive slowing on brief bedside testing. Vitals stable.

Plan:
- Education provided regarding sleep regularity, stimulus control around screens, and hydration; she is willing to trial structured sleep scheduling, including screen curfew by 23:00 and consistent wake time.
- Commence topiramate 25 mg nightly for two weeks, then consider gradual titration if tolerated. Counselling given on potential adverse effects (cognitive slowing, word-finding difficulty, paraesthesia, weight loss, mood change, risk of kidney stones, acute ocular symptoms) and the importance of adequate hydration.
- Arrange epilepsy nurse telephone follow-up at 4–6 weeks to assess tolerability and seizure pattern; consider dose increase depending on response.
- Provide a seizure diary template with prompts to record bedtime, screen use after 22:00, caffeine/energy drink intake, and event details.
- Discussed alternative options for future consideration if needed, including zonisamide, perampanel, or VNS, depending on response and tolerability to current approach.
- Request updated MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and a sleep-deprived EEG given the current pattern of nocturnally clustered events.

I will review her routinely in clinic in three months, sooner if there is any deterioration, injuries, medication intolerance, or if early-morning continuation of clusters becomes more prolonged.

Yours sincerely

Events remain brief, typically seconds to under a minute, with preserved awareness in some but not all instances; there has been no associated injury, and there is no new focal neurological deficit reported. She denies medication side effects beyond mild morning fatigue over the past fortnight.

There have been no emergency department attendances, no use of rescue medication, and no status events. Triggers continue to include poor sleep and intercurrent illness as previously described. A seizure diary was reviewed and corroborates the above pattern.

On examination today, general and neurological examinations were unremarkable. Vital signs were stable. No cutaneous stigmata or focal signs were elicited. 

Investigations: No new imaging since last MRI (unchanged). Prior EEGs remain non-diagnostic with non-specific changes. Routine bloods last month were within reference limits, including bicarbonate and lipid profile as monitored for dietary therapy.

Plan: 
- We will collate and share today’s seizure frequency data and the diet log with the supervising dietary team to ensure coordinated care. 
- No immediate medication changes were made today; we will seek clarification from the referring prescriber regarding the rationale for the recent dose increase given the reported change in frequency. 
- Safety advice and seizure first aid were reinforced. She will continue the seizure diary and bring home ketone readings to the next review. 
- We will arrange a follow-up in 8–10 weeks, or sooner if there is any escalation in event duration, clustering, injury, or concerns from the family or school. 

Yours sincerely

Dear Dr Susan,  

Daniel Cooper, DOB: 12-07-1987, Hospital No: U428516 NHS No. 7482193650  
22 Brookside Mews, London, NW1 4AL 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy with monthly seizure clusters; remote history of mild traumatic brain injury; migraine without aura; vitamin D insufficiency; ex-smoker. 

Medication: Sodium Valproate 500 mg in the morning and 1000 mg at night.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily (titrated from August 2025).  
Cholecalciferol 1000 IU daily.  
Sumatriptan 50 mg as required for migraine (maximum 2 per attack).  

I reviewed Daniel Cooper in clinic today accompanied by his partner. He has remained generally well and is engaging in regular exercise, reporting improved sleep routine over the last month. He notes no adverse cognitive effects from the introduction of lamotrigine; mild nausea in the first week resolved spontaneously. There have been no new focal neurological symptoms. He works part time as a delivery coordinator and has self-reported good adherence to medication using a phone reminder system. Remote history of mild traumatic brain injury was noted from a cycling incident in early adulthood without lasting focal deficits. 

Seizures: He reports Monthly clusters, typically 3 - 10 seizures over 24 h, characterised by generalised tonic-clonic seizures preceded by brief generalised myoclonic jerks on waking. He and his partner maintain a seizure diary that confirms this pattern since June 2025. Between clusters, there are no intercurrent seizures and no clear focal onset features. There is a consistent association with sleep deprivation and missed evening meals. No injuries, tongue lacerations are occasional, and post-ictal confusion resolves within an hour. He has not required emergency department attendance in the past six months. Based on semiology, diary review, and prior EEG showing generalised spike–wave discharges, the overall impression remains that he has generalised epilepsy. 

Examination: Alert and oriented; normal speech and language. Cranial nerves intact. Motor power full throughout; tone and coordination normal. Gait steady. No tremor or ataxia. 

Investigations: 
- EEG (April 2025): Generalised 3–4 Hz spike–wave bursts with photoparoxysmal response; no focal abnormalities.  
- MRI brain (March 2024): Normal for age; no focal cortical dysplasia or post-traumatic changes.  
- Bloods (September 2025): FBC, U&E, LFTs all within reference; vitamin D 24 ng/mL (insufficient).  

Plan:  
- Continue sodium valproate 500 mg am/1000 mg pm.  
- Continue lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily; consider cautious up-titration to 150 mg twice daily if cluster frequency persists over the next two months without adverse effects.  
- Provide buccal midazolam 10 mg as a rescue medication for prolonged seizures (>5 minutes) or for recurrent seizures within a short period, with training for partner on administration.  
- Lifestyle measures: prioritise regular sleep, consistent evening meals, and hydration; continue diary documentation of clusters and potential triggers.  
- Safety: avoid unsupervised swimming and heights; driving not permitted in accordance with DVLA guidance until seizure freedom meets required intervals.  
- Check vitamin D level in three months while on supplementation.  
- Review in clinic in three months, or sooner if clusters increase in frequency or severity.  

Yours sincerely

Medication: 

- Levetiracetam 1,500 mg twice daily, continued.  
- Clobazam 10 mg at night, as adjunct.  
- Previously tried: Sodium valproate (weight gain and tremor), Lamotrigine (rash), Topiramate (cognitive slowing), Carbamazepine (worsened balance), and Perampanel (mood change).  

History since last review: 

He attended with his parents, who provide most of the day-to-day observations. Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures three nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. They feel his recovery time after the larger convulsions is shorter on the current combination, and he has had fewer tongue bites. No reported status episodes. 

Sleep and lifestyle: 

High caffeine intake was acknowledged; he routinely consumes strong coffee and energy drinks, particularly after lunch and into the evening, which he and his parents suspect may relate to nights with poorer sleep and increased clustering. He reports irregular sleep–wake timing on weekends. No alcohol. No recreational drugs. 

Examination and tests: 

General and neurological examinations today were unchanged from baseline. No focal deficits noted. Previous MRI reported structurally unremarkable findings. Serial EEGs have shown non-specific abnormalities but without a consistent lateralising pattern. Bloods last month (U&E, LFTs, FBC, vitamin D) were within reference limits. 

Assessment and plan: 

Given the stable overall pattern and the family’s perception of modest day-to-day benefit on the present regimen, I have left treatment unaltered. He will continue Levetiracetam 1,500 mg twice daily and Clobazam 10 mg at night. We discussed practical measures around sleep hygiene and stimulant use; he and his parents intend to gradually reduce caffeinated drinks, especially after midday, and to keep a simple seizure and sleep diary to help identify any temporal relationships. They are content with this approach for now. 

I have provided a rescue midazolam buccal preparation for prolonged events as previously, with no change to instructions. The family understands to seek urgent help if there is any prolonged convulsion or notable change in pattern. 

Follow-up: 

We will review him in six months, or sooner if there is deterioration or problematic adverse effects. 

Yours sincerely

- Continue Levetiracetam 1,000 mg bd

- Driving: patient not currently driving; to follow DVLA guidance and remain off driving until seizure-free per regulations  
- Consider referral to the epilepsy surgery MDT if disabling seizures persist despite optimisation  
- Safety advice provided regarding bathing, heights, hot surfaces, and nocturnal supervision  

I reviewed John in clinic today regarding focal epilepsy. He describes brief focal aware seizures characterised by rising epigastric sensation, déjà vu, and transient speech arrest, occasionally followed by right-hand automatisms and, more rarely, progression to bilateral tonic-clonic activity. He reports a current seizure frequency of 17 per month, typically clustering around periods of sleep deprivation and high work-related stress. He has found avoidance of alcohol helpful and is now using a structured sleep schedule. 

There is a remote history of childhood febrile seizures without neurological sequelae. Adult-onset focal seizures began in late 2023. Prior trials included Lamotrigine up to 200 mg bd, which was discontinued due to rash, and Carbamazepine up to 600 mg bd, which reduced frequency but caused significant dizziness and hyponatraemia; both medications were stopped in consultation with his previous team. 

We discussed treatment options. Given the MRI and EEG concordance suggesting a left mesial temporal focus, I have recommended optimising current therapy by titrating Lacosamide to 150 mg twice daily if tolerated, while continuing Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily. He will maintain a detailed seizure diary and track potential triggers. A rescue plan using buccal Midazolam for prolonged seizures was reinforced. 

If disabling seizures persist at a rate similar to the current 17 per month despite dose optimisation and adherence, I will refer him to the epilepsy surgery multidisciplinary team for further evaluation, including consideration of prolonged video-EEG telemetry and neuropsychological assessment. We also reviewed DVLA regulations; he is not currently driving and understands requirements for resumption. 

I will review him again in three months, or earlier if there is any deterioration or adverse effects from the dose increase. 

With best wishes,

She reports clusters of brief nocturnal warning sensations followed by loss of awareness on three occasions in September and two further events in early October. This worsening appears to coincide with a change to regular night shifts and curtailed sleep, and she feels that disrupted sleep patterns reliably precede the events by 24–48 hours. She also notes increased fatigue and difficulty recovering after each episode. 

Her partner witnessed the most recent event and described a blank stare, unresponsiveness for approximately one minute, and subsequent confusion lasting 10–15 minutes without tongue biting or incontinence. There have been no injuries. She has not been driving. Headaches have been more frequent after nights on duty, and she has been taking simple analgesics no more than three times per week. She denies alcohol excess or recreational drug use. Menses are regular and she has not noticed a cyclical pattern. 

On review today, neurological examination is unremarkable. Routine bloods from last month (FBC, U&E, LFTs) were within reference ranges. She has not previously had prolonged sleep deprivation provocation in EEG, and her last EEG two years ago was non-diagnostic. MRI brain (2019) was reported normal. 

The pattern she describes suggests an increase in susceptibility in the context of disrupted circadian rhythm and insufficient sleep, amounting to a sleep-disruption–linked escalation in events. We discussed practical strategies to improve sleep opportunity around shift work, including protected wind-down time, consistent wake routines post-night shift, and minimising consecutive nights where possible. She will explore temporary adjustments to her rota with occupational health. 

In view of the recent deterioration, I have recommended we optimise treatment. We will: 
- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily for the moment. She will keep a detailed seizure and sleep diary. 
- Arrange an urgent sleep-deprived EEG and repeat MRI brain with epilepsy protocol. 
- Check trough levetiracetam level, vitamin D, and repeat U&E/LFTs. 
- Provide intranasal midazolam as a rescue plan for prolonged events (>5 minutes) or recurrent episodes within a short period, with partner instruction sheet supplied. 

I have advised strict sleep hygiene, avoidance of missed doses, and no driving in line with DVLA guidance following recent events. She is aware to seek urgent help if seizures become more frequent, prolonged, or if any injury occurs. 

I will review her in eight weeks, or sooner if her situation worsens. I will update you when the investigations are available. 

Yours sincerely

Seizure disorder; classification currently under ongoing evaluation 

Aetiology: 

No definitive structural or genetic cause identified to date; investigations ongoing

Mild allergic rhinitis (seasonal); vitamin D insufficiency on supplementation 

Management Plan: 

- Continue current maintenance Levetiracetam at 1,000 mg twice daily; patient reports acceptable tolerability (mild irritability only).

This patient works in a cold-chain warehouse where sub-zero temperatures and the need for gloves are standard. He describes two distinct event types. He denies clear triggers but notes that episodes are more frequent during rapid task switching and when moving between freezer aisles. There is no associated tongue biting or incontinence reported. Second, he experiences occasional brief staring with unresponsiveness, lasting 10–15 seconds, about once every two weeks, sometimes followed by a mild frontal headache. He carries buccal midazolam; however, he reports that low ambient temperatures and the necessity for insulated gloves increase the time to retrieve and administer the device. We discussed practical measures: keeping the prefilled syringes in a pocketed insulated pouch within recommended storage parameters, positioning a second kit at the end of the aisle where he most frequently works, and ensuring two trained colleagues are consistently rostered nearby. He will undertake an updated workplace risk assessment with his manager. 

There have been no recent head injuries. Sleep is fragmented due to early shifts; he will aim for more regular sleep where feasible. Alcohol intake is minimal. No recreational drugs. Family history is non-contributory. Basic bloods last month were within reference ranges apart from low vitamin D now supplemented.

Epilepsy

Medication: Initiated Levetiracetam August 2025  
Levetiracetam 750mg twice daily (film-coated)  
Clobazam 5mg nocte as rescue for clusters (patient-reported, used intermittently)

Therapeutic Monitoring:  
Levetiracetam level (29 September 2025): 14 µg/mL (reference 12–46). Dose at sampling: 750mg twice daily. 

Seizures: The patient describes combined seizure types:  
- Generalised events: brief generalised tonic–clonic seizures without warning, witnessed by partner, with tongue-bite on two occasions.  
- Focal events: focal impaired-awareness seizures with behavioural arrest and right-hand automatisms, lasting 30–60 seconds, with post-event confusion.  
Frequency currently reported as several episodes per day, predominantly focal events, with occasional generalised breakthroughs (approximately once weekly). 

I reviewed Daniel Harper today via video consultation for a routine follow-up. They report improved sleep since starting levetiracetam but ongoing daytime clusters of focal impaired-awareness spells, described as “zoning out” with lip-smacking and right-hand fumbling, occurring in short flurries, several episodes per day. There is a history of rare generalised tonic–clonic seizures, most recently two weeks ago, occurring in the early morning after a missed evening dose. No clear photosensitivity. Triggers noted include sleep deprivation and high work-related stress. No recent head injury or intercurrent infection. Auras include a rising epigastric sensation and brief déjà vu. 

Safety/Function: No current driving (patient confirms DVLA notified). No recent injuries beyond mild tongue trauma. Uses a smartphone seizure diary; partner provides collateral history. 

Investigations: 
- MRI brain with epilepsy protocol (June 2025): normal.  
- EEG (May 2025): generalised spike–wave discharges and independent left temporal sharp waves, supporting both generalised and focal epilepsy physiology.  
- CMP/CBP (September 2025): within normal limits.

- Continue levetiracetam 750mg twice daily for now; adherence reinforced.  
- Patient to maintain seizure diary noting time-of-day, precipitating factors, and whether generalised or focal; this will help quantify the several episodes per day pattern more precisely.  
- We discussed potential escalation if focal clusters persist, including cautious titration of levetiracetam or addition of lamotrigine or lacosamide at next review; patient understood and agrees this will be revisited after two further weeks of data.  
- Clobazam 5mg nocte remains reserved for short cluster periods, as previously instructed; patient will notify us if used more than two consecutive nights.  
- Lifestyle: regular sleep schedule, hydration, and stress-reduction advised (patient-reported measures). 

Follow-up: Telephone review in 3 weeks to reassess frequency (aiming to reduce from several episodes per day) and tolerability. Urgent contact advised if generalised seizures increase or injuries occur. 

I can confirm the clinical picture remains of combined generalised and focal epilepsy, with daily focal impaired-awareness seizures and occasional generalised tonic–clonic events. 

Yours sincerely

Dear Dr Amelia Rhodes
Consultant Neurologist,

Mr Peter Langley, DOB: 14-03-1954, Hospital No: KCH782411 NHS No. 9473165820
Flat 2, 18 Rowan Court, Dulwich, London SE21 8JT

Thank you for asking me to review your patient, whom I saw in the neurology clinic today. He is a newly retired teacher who lives alone, maintains a regular daily routine, and reports reduced social contact since retirement. He attended unaccompanied and was able to give a clear account of his history.

He describes two distinct seizure types consistent with combined generalised and focal epilepsy. The focal events begin with a rising epigastric sensation and a brief pause with loss of awareness, during which he is noted to stare and fumble with nearby objects for up to 60–90 seconds, followed by several minutes of confusion. Separately, he has generalised tonic–clonic seizures that typically occur from sleep or in the early morning without a clear prodrome. There is no consistent trigger identified, though he reports that missed meals and fragmented sleep have occasionally preceded events.

Following introduction of lamotrigine, there has been a clear improvement in control, with just nine generalised tonic-clonic seizures documented this year to date. He also reports a reduction in the frequency of focal impaired-awareness episodes, now occurring roughly once every three to four weeks, usually clustering after periods of stress or disrupted sleep. He keeps a written seizure diary, which correlates with this history.

There is no history of status epilepticus. Post-ictally he experiences myalgia and headache for several hours, but there is no prolonged confusion beyond the immediate recovery period. He denies tongue-biting outside of the generalised events and has had two minor falls this year without injury. There is no current use of alcohol or recreational drugs, and he remains independent with activities of daily living. He has limited social contact but is open to community support should it be available.

Current anti-seizure medication is lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily, well tolerated without rash, dizziness, or diplopia. He is not on any other anti-seizure therapy at present, and there have been no recent medication changes. He is adherent and uses a pill organiser. Sleep hygiene is reasonable; he typically retires at 22:30 and rises at 06:30.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal language, intact cranial nerves, normal tone and power throughout, symmetrical reflexes, and a normal gait. There were no focal neurological deficits detected. Cardiovascular and general examination were unremarkable.

In summary, the clinical picture is of combined generalised and focal epilepsy, with both focal impaired-awareness seizures and separate generalised tonic–clonic seizures. Seizure control has improved on lamotrigine, with a clear reduction in generalised events this year as above. We discussed ongoing risk reduction, including consistent sleep routine, regular meals, and maintaining the seizure diary. He is not currently driving and understands DVLA guidance as applicable. I have provided information on local peer support groups to address social isolation, which he welcomed.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily; no immediate dose change given current benefit and tolerability.
- Arrange routine EEG and updated MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) to further characterise both generalised and focal features.
- Check lamotrigine trough level, FBC, U&Es, LFTs, and vitamin D at next phlebotomy.
- Reinforce seizure diary and safety advice at home; provide written advice on when to seek urgent care.
- Follow-up in clinic in 6 months, or sooner should there be any deterioration or clustering of seizures.

Thank you for your referral. Please do not hesitate to contact me if any issues arise before the next appointment.

Yours sincerely

Dear Dr Helen Carter

Daniel Everett, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K742119 NHS No. 9075314682  
12 Brook View, Herne Hill, London SE24 0AB

Thank you for referring this patient for review following several recent events suggestive of ongoing seizure activity. I met with him and his partner in clinic today. He reports a remote history of a mild traumatic brain injury in adolescence without documented loss of consciousness longer than a few minutes and no known neurosurgical intervention at the time. There is no family history of seizures to his knowledge. He does not drive.

Over the past four months there has been an increase in events described by the patient and corroborated by his partner. In Feb two generalised tonic-clonic seizures were reported. In Mar a nocturnal seizure occurred, and in Apr another during respite care. The daytime events were said to begin without a consistent warning; witnesses describe bilateral stiffening and rhythmic jerks lasting approximately 90–120 seconds with a bitten lateral tongue on one occasion and post-event confusion for 30–45 minutes. The nocturnal event was inferred from a bed partner report of witnessed jerking, vocalisation, and subsequent morning myalgia, tongue soreness, and headache. The episode during respite care was recorded in the facility log with similar semiology and a comparable period of post-event disorientation.

Between events he reports intermittent fatigue and reduced concentration but no clear focal neurological symptoms. He is currently taking levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily as his sole antiseizure medicine, which he started eight months ago; adherence is reported as good with a single missed evening dose in late March. He denies alcohol excess or recreational drug use. Sleep has been fragmented since February owing to anxiety about further events. There is no history provided to suggest consistent triggers.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with fluent speech. Cranial nerves were intact, power 5/5 in all limbs, tone normal, and coordination and gait without focal abnormality. Fundoscopy was unremarkable. No cutaneous stigmata were present. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were normal. There were no features to suggest an intercurrent infection.

Prior investigations: a routine EEG performed two years ago was reported as within normal limits. A CT head following an event in 2017 showed no acute abnormality; there is no prior MRI available for review. Routine blood tests last month (FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, CRP) were within reference ranges; magnesium was not checked.

Impression: The clinical description is of recurrent convulsive events with post-event confusion and a probable nocturnal event. The overall pattern remains difficult to classify on clinical grounds alone, and the contribution of the remote mild traumatic brain injury is uncertain. The recent clustering across February to April warrants further evaluation and optimisation of management.

Plan discussed with the patient and partner:
- Arrange prolonged ambulatory EEG with sleep capture to characterise events, and request MRI brain with an epilepsy protocol given the absence of prior MRI.
- Repeat routine bloods including magnesium and vitamin D; check levetiracetam trough level for adherence context.
- Provide seizure first-aid advice and written information; partner is comfortable administering lateral positioning and timing events. Rescue midazolam was not deemed necessary today pending further characterisation, but we will revisit after monitoring if events escalate.
- Reinforce safety guidance: avoid heights, unsupervised swimming, and operate machinery with caution. Driving restrictions as per DVLA Group 1 apply; he states he is not currently driving and understands the need to inform the DVLA and his insurer.
- Sleep hygiene advice offered; patient-reported anxiety will be reviewed after investigations and he is open to brief psychological support if needed.

We will review in clinic once investigations are completed, or sooner if there is any deterioration. Please let me know if you would like any additional tests arranged locally.

Yours sincerely

Dear Dr Tomas 
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K917204 NHS No. 7489203156  
Flat 12, Mariners Court, 8 Dockside Way, London, SE16 3AB

Thank you for asking me to review this gentleman. We conducted a planned telephone consultation while he was onshore between offshore rotations. He reports a longstanding history of generalised epilepsy. He may go four to five days without seizures, but when they happen he often has them in batches, with two - three occurring within 24 hours. He and his partner describe typical events as abrupt loss of awareness with generalised stiffening and rhythmic jerking lasting around 60–90 seconds, followed by post-ictal confusion for up to 30 minutes. He occasionally bites his tongue and has had one shoulder strain in the past but no recent injuries. There is no consistent focal onset aura reported.

He works offshore on a two-weeks-on, two-weeks-off rotation. Seizures tend to cluster either at the end of a run of night shifts or within the first 48 hours after returning onshore, which he attributes to sleep disruption and variable meal times. He denies alcohol excess and states he avoids caffeine late in the day. He uses a medication alarm and a written seizure log while offshore. Occupational Health have placed him on restricted duties when offshore; he confirms he does not undertake safety-critical tasks on days following a seizure.

He is currently taking valproate 1,000 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 1,500 mg twice daily with good adherence. No clear adverse effects are reported beyond mild irritability during periods of poor sleep. He has rescue buccal midazolam available but has not needed to use it in the last three months. He has not driven and understands DVLA regulations. He last had a generalised tonic–clonic seizure 10 days ago, with two events occurring the same day, and none since. 

Past EEGs (most recently last year) showed generalised spike-and-wave discharges consistent with a generalised epilepsy syndrome; prior MRI brain was normal. There is no family history of epilepsy known. He has had no recent infections, head injuries, or changes in prescribed medication.

Impression: Generalised epilepsy with clustering related to sleep disruption and rota transitions. Current regimen is broadly effective with intermittent breakthrough clusters as described above. We discussed optimising sleep hygiene during shift transitions and maintaining strict regularity of doses while offshore. He prefers to continue the present regimen for now. We agreed to continue telephone reviews when he is onshore, with the option of bringing him for a routine EEG update in the next 6–9 months if breakthrough clusters increase. He will contact the clinic sooner if he experiences prolonged seizures, increased frequency, or any injuries.

Plan:
- Continue valproate 1,000 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 1,500 mg twice daily.
- Maintain rescue midazolam for prolonged seizures (>5 minutes) and cluster management per prior plan.
- Reinforce sleep and shift-transition strategies; continue seizure diary.
- Occupational Health restrictions to remain unchanged offshore.
- Telephone review during his next onshore period in approximately 8–10 weeks, or earlier if seizures escalate.

Yours sincerely

Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness, likely temporal lobe onset (MRI 2023 non-lesional; EEG 2024 left temporal intermittent rhythmic delta activity)

Current Medication:

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily
Lacosamide 150 mg twice daily
Midazolam 5 mg buccal rescue as required for prolonged events

Plan:

- Increase lacosamide to 200 mg twice daily, monitor tolerability (dizziness, diplopia, PR interval)
- Continue levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily for now; consider switch to brivaracetam at next review if no improvement
- Arrange 48–72 hour ambulatory EEG to better characterise events and correlate with diary
- Refer to epilepsy specialist nurse for rescue plan reinforcement and adherence support; ensure midazolam supply and training up to date
- Discuss driving regulations: patient not currently driving and understands DVLA guidance
- Consider tertiary referral for presurgical evaluation if inadequate response after optimisation (including consideration of lamotrigine or cenobamate)
- Safety advice given regarding bathing, heights, and use of machinery; SUDEP risk discussed
- Follow-up in 8–10 weeks or sooner if further escalation

I reviewed Michael Harris in clinic today. He reports a clear deterioration over the past four months despite good adherence to levetiracetam and lacosamide. Seizure pattern appears largely unchanged, with events approximately every three days. Occasionally, frequency escalates to daily, and he required midazolam rescue in July and September. His partner describes typical episodes beginning with a rising epigastric sensation and deja vu, followed by impaired awareness, lip-smacking, and right hand dystonia lasting 60–90 seconds, with post-ictal confusion and fatigue for up to 30 minutes. There have been no generalised tonic–clonic seizures.

He has kept a diary indicating clustering under stress and sleep deprivation. He denies missed doses and has not noted specific dietary or alcohol triggers. There is no new neurological deficit. We reviewed prior investigations: MRI brain (3T, 2023) non-lesional; routine EEG in 2024 demonstrated left temporal abnormalities consistent with focal epilepsy. Given limited benefit from the current regimen, I have advised up-titration of lacosamide as above and arranged ambulatory EEG to guide further optimisation. We also refreshed the rescue plan and reinforced seizure first aid for his partner. He is not driving and understands that this must continue until seizure control is re-established in line with DVLA guidance.

I will reassess after the medication adjustment and EEG. If there is insufficient response, we will consider medication switch and discuss referral to the regional surgical workup pathway.

With best wishes,

Recurrent seizure episodes; current classification not established. Most recent event during commercial flight; relationship to situational stressors and sleep loss considered.

No structural lesion identified on previous MRI; further evaluation ongoing. 

Other Medical Problems: 

Mild generalised anxiety symptoms, particularly related to flying; seasonal allergic rhinitis. No known drug allergies.

Since their last review, the patient reports one breakthrough episode on 12/09/2025 while on a late-evening flight from London to Lisbon (ï¿½row 18C). Witness description consistent with brief generalised stiffening followed by rhythmic movements lasting approximately 90 seconds, with post-event confusion ~15 minutes. Cabin crew initiated standard first aid; no injuries. No in-flight alcohol was consumed; however, the patient had slept poorly for two nights and had missed lunch, with noted high pre-flight anxiety. They describe a pattern over the past two years where seizures tend to occur in the context of disrupted sleep and heightened stress around travel rather than after alcohol ingestion (Ã©"stress-and-sleep-linked events"). Day-to-day, there have been no episodes in the preceding eight months, and there is no history of early-morning clusters. 

They tolerate Levetiracetam without mood change or irritability. Routine bloods in July 2025 were within normal ranges. We discussed that the observed pattern suggests vulnerability around circadian disruption and situational stress; accordingly, we will combine sleep-optimised strategies with diagnostic clarification via ambulatory EEG. I will update you following the EEG or sooner should there be any further events. 

Yours sincerely

Dear Dr A. R. Patel
Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: H928374 NHS No. 8037164925
Flat 3, 29 Willow Court, Peckham, London SE15 4JT 

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset, drug-resistant; history of mixed seizure types with nocturnal predominance; cumulative fatigue with perceived worsening in the context of irregular shift work in a care setting with frequent patient transfers.

Imaging/EEG: MRI 2017 reported no definite epileptogenic lesion. Prior routine EEGs with intermittent left temporal sharp transients; no definitive lateralising lesion captured on brief recordings.

Previous medication: Lamotrigine (ongoing), Levetiracetam (behavioural irritability), Carbamazepine (dizziness, hyponatraemia), Sodium Valproate (weight gain, tremor), Clobazam (sedation), Lacosamide (no clear benefit), Topiramate (word-finding difficulty), Zonisamide (renal colic), Perampanel (mood change), Pregabalin (somnolence), Rufinamide (GI upset). Vagus nerve stimulation discussed in past but not pursued.

Current medication: Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily; Midazolam 10 mg buccal PRN for prolonged convulsive seizures; Vitamin D supplementation.

I reviewed Daniel Harris in clinic today, accompanied by his father. He works in a residential care home where breaks are irregular and he is often involved in frequent patient transfers. He reports accumulating fatigue across the week with poorer sleep consolidation. He and his parents have noticed increased recovery time after seizures, more bruising from sudden drops, and reduced confidence mobilising unaccompanied. Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 2 - 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. However, functionally he is deteriorating: he is missing more work days, requires more supervision at home, and has described worsening morning confusion and headaches consistent with sleep fragmentation. He denies new infectious triggers or recent head injury. No alcohol or recreational drugs. He uses a smartphone seizure diary which corroborates clustered drops particularly after late shifts.

Examination today: Alert and orientated. Speech fluent. No focal neurological deficit. No tongue laceration currently; healing ecchymosis over right forearm consistent with recent drop. Blood pressure 122/74 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular. BMI 24.1 kg/m2.

Risk/safety: He reports two nocturnal convulsions this past week without supervision as his parents were away one night; he used his bed sensor alarm on the second occasion. No status epilepticus. No recent aspiration, but he describes biting inside cheek. No known pregnancy considerations. Driving: he is not driving and understands ongoing DVLA restrictions.

Work and function: Care setting duties with manual handling appear to aggravate fatigue and perceived seizure burden. We discussed reasonable adjustments at work (regular protected breaks, reduced overnight duties, avoiding lone working for transfers) and he will raise this with occupational health.

Plan:
- Continue Lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily for now; adherence good. We discussed options including revisiting VNS and consideration of inpatient video-EEG telemetry to clarify seizure semiology and assess candidacy for neuromodulation. He is open to VNS; I have provided written information and will refer to the device clinic.
- Arrange 5–7 day home sleep study and actigraphy via community sleep service to quantify sleep fragmentation, given nocturnal predominance and daytime fatigue.
- Bloods: U&Es, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D, FBC, lamotrigine trough level, and HbA1c (rule out metabolic contributors to fatigue).
- Safety: reinforce use of bed rails as appropriate, padded headboard, avoid baths unsupervised, ensure buccal midazolam is in-date and that family trained; employer to review manual handling plan.
- Occupational health letter to support phased adjustments including structured breaks and minimising consecutive late shifts.
- Seizure diary: continue electronic log with tags for shift pattern, sleep duration, perceived stress, and meal times to correlate with clusters.
- Follow-up: telephone review by epilepsy nurse specialist in 4 weeks to reassess clusters and injuries; clinic review with me in 3 months or earlier if further deterioration (injuries, prolonged convulsions, status, or intolerable side-effects).

I emphasise that while the absolute seizure pattern appears long-standing, his functional trajectory is deteriorating with cumulative fatigue, more injuries from drop attacks, and increasing post-ictal recovery time in the context of irregular work patterns. We will proceed with the above investigations and VNS pathway discussion to mitigate further decline.

Yours sincerely

Diagnoses: 

Seizure disorder (classification not established). 

I reviewed Jordan Ellis in clinic today. History is patient-reported as there were no accompanying witnesses. Jordan Ellis lives on a houseboat/mobile home, reporting intermittent power outages which at times disrupt charging of personal devices (including seizure alert watch) and daily routines. 

Seizure description is variably reported: sudden loss of awareness with brief stiffening and subsequent confusion on some occasions; at other times, an abrupt pause with lip-smacking and fumbling behaviours lasting approximately 60–90 seconds, followed by mild headache and fatigue. There have also been rare reports of a brief warning sensation described as a rising epigastric feeling. No clear diurnal pattern. Triggers are uncertain; poor sleep during outages and missed meals are suspected. 

Frequency: over the past three months, the patient notes events occurring at intervals ranging 7 - 10 days, though a two-week gap was recalled once during a period of improved sleep. The monitoring app on the phone shows sporadic entries consistent with this pattern, but gaps coincide with times when the watch was uncharged due to power issues. 

Current treatment: no antiseizure medicines are being taken at present; the patient has declined immediate initiation pending further investigation. No rescue medicines held. No VNS or RNS in situ. The patient uses a smartwatch-based detector, though charging is inconsistent. Ã‚Â· Occasional use of a meditation app is reported; no cannabis or alcohol. 

Examination today was unremarkable: alert, oriented, no focal neurological deficits; BP 118/74, pulse 72 regular; normal gait. 

Investigations to date (patient-reported): a routine EEG done approximately two years ago elsewhere was said to be “borderline normal”; no report available. No prior brain MRI according to the patient. 

Plan discussed today (patient preferences noted): 
- Arrange MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and sleep-deprived EEG for classification and risk assessment. 
- Provide written first-aid guidance and safety advice tailored to living on a boat/mobile home, including cooking supervision, shower rather than bath, and mooring-side precautions. 
- Offer antiseizure medication initiation after investigations; patient prefers to defer for now but agrees to revisit this if frequency increases or injuries occur. 
- Issue a simple paper seizure diary to complement the watch logs during power outages. 
- Discussed driving regulations and the need to abstain from driving in view of ongoing events; patient states they are not currently driving. 
- Signposted to community epilepsy nursing for practical support and to local services regarding intermittent electricity supply. 

Follow-up: I will review Jordan Ellis in clinic in 10–12 weeks, or sooner if events cluster more frequently than the current pattern of intervals ranging 7 - 10 days, any injury occurs, or there is prolonged confusion. 

Yours sincerely

Recurrent seizures with recent increase in events, currently exhibiting a pattern consistent with episodes clustering around periods of inconsistent antiseizure medication adherence and alcohol exposure. 

Aetiology: 

Unclear at present; further evaluation ongoing. 

Other Medical Problems: 

Generalised anxiety symptoms reported by patient; intermittent insomnia. No known drug allergies. Vitamin D insufficiency treated with colecalciferol 1,000 IU daily. 

Management Plan: 

- Continue current antiseizure regimen: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily. Reinforced importance of consistent dosing and avoiding brand switching.

Since the last visit, this patient reports a noticeable worsening in seizure control. They describe several episodes over the past six weeks occurring particularly after late evenings out and on mornings following social events. They acknowledge increased social pressure and higher alcohol intake recently, which has coincided with delays or omissions of scheduled medication doses. The current pattern could be summarised as breakthrough events linked to irregular ASM intake and alcohol, with two episodes requiring overnight recovery due to postictal confusion but no injuries reported. No new focal neurological symptoms between events. 

Examination today was unremarkable; cognition intact, gait steady, and no lateralising signs. Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm, regular. We discussed that the priority is to stabilise routine and reduce alcohol exposure while we proceed with further investigations to clarify the underlying cause of the deterioration.

From: Dr Emma Richardson (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 19 December 2014 10:15
To: occupational.health@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Naveen Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Laura Bennett (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Workplace reasonable adjustments

Hi HR Team

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Primary generalised epilepsy

Context:

The patient is the primary caregiver for young children, with night-time sleep often interrupted. This can affect daytime alertness and consistency of routine.

Employer Reasonable Adjustments (non-clinical):

1. Flexible scheduling: Offer a stable start time after 09:30 or a flexible window to accommodate disrupted nights. Allow occasional remote work when sleep has been significantly interrupted.
2. Breaks and rest periods: Ensure access to a quiet space for short restorative breaks (10–15 minutes) twice daily, with the option for an additional break following a disrupted night.
3. Workload and task allocation: Prioritise tasks requiring higher concentration earlier in the day; avoid clustering prolonged, uninterrupted tasks. Permit temporary reduction of non-essential duties during periods of increased caregiving demands.
4. Environmental considerations: Provide consistent lighting, minimise excessive screen glare, and allow the use of anti-glare filters. Maintain a calm workspace with reduced unnecessary noise where feasible.
5. Predictable rotas: Give at least two weeks’ notice for rota changes. Avoid late-evening shifts and back-to-back early starts following late finishes.
6. Safety planning: Agree a simple workplace support plan including a named contact, safe access to rest areas, and clear procedures for colleagues to follow if the patient becomes unwell.
7. Leave and appointments: Permit short-notice compassionate or carer’s leave when childcare issues arise. Support time off for medical appointments without penalising attendance records.
8. Training and awareness: Provide basic staff guidance on supporting employees with long-term neurological conditions, focusing on respectful communication and privacy.

These adjustments are intended to support sustained employment, reduce fatigue-related risks, and promote wellbeing for an individual with generalised epilepsy who is also a primary caregiver.

With best wishes

Thank you for asking me to review this patient regarding his established epilepsy and occupational considerations. He attended with his partner who corroborated the history. 

He has a diagnosis of generalised epilepsy. His typical events are described as an abrupt loss of awareness with generalised stiffening followed by rhythmic jerking, brief vocalisation at onset in some episodes, and a post-ictal phase of confusion and headache. There are no consistent focal features reported before or after the events. He and his partner report that the seizures occur every four months, with the last event taking place on site in the loading area. On that occasion, colleagues followed the workplace plan and kept him safe. There was no injury. 

Known triggers are sleep deprivation and missed meals during busy shifts; he denies alcohol overuse. He wears a smartwatch that flags elevated heart rate around exertion but there has been no reliable prodrome. He does not drive. He reports excellent adherence to his medication and brings pharmacy records aligned with this. He stores his rescue midazolam at work; however, he notes that cold-room conditions and gloves make unfastening the case slower, a point corroborated by the warehouse first-aid lead via the patient’s written report. 

Examination today is unremarkable. Neurological exam is normal between events. No features to suggest a progressive process. 

Plan:
- Continue current anti-seizure regimen as tolerability is good and seizure burden is low at every four months.
- Provide a duplicate, easy-open rescue pack for use outside the cold rooms and signpost the employer to review access within their safety protocol, as per patient-reported concerns.
- Reinforce sleep and meal regularity around shifts.
- Issue an updated individual seizure management plan for the workplace with clear timings and roles.

Recurrent unprovoked seizures (first presentation 2023)

Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily

Plan:

Safety counselling reinforced regarding bathing, heights, and machinery 
Driving advice per DVLA Group 1 standards; patient currently not driving and understands need to notify DVLA if applicable 
Seizure diary to be maintained; consider reduction of ASM only after a minimum of two seizure-free years with shared decision-making 
Arrange repeat renal profile and mood screening in three months 

I reviewed Amelia Johnson in my clinic today. They describe a history of intermittent collapse events beginning in late 2023, characterised by sudden loss of awareness, brief tonic stiffening reported by witnesses, and several minutes of post-event confusion. No consistent focal warning has been identified. Initial MRI brain (2023) was unremarkable, and an ambulatory EEG in June 2024 did not capture definitive epileptiform discharges. There is a family history negative for seizures, and no clear provoking factors were identified at onset. 

Since commencing Levetiracetam in July 2024, there has been seizure cessation following initiation of last ASM. The patient has remained seizure-free to the present review. They report good adherence, no notable mood disturbance, and no daytime somnolence. Headaches that were present around the time of initial events have settled. 

Socially, the patient is awaiting relocation from temporary accommodation; crowded living conditions were noted and they feel this has intermittently impacted sleep regularity. We discussed sleep hygiene, stress management, and the importance of consistent medication timing despite current housing circumstances. They are currently not driving and understand statutory guidance. 

On examination today, general and focused neurological examination was normal. Blood tests from July 2025 (U&E, LFT, FBC) were within reference ranges. We agreed to continue current therapy. If seizure freedom persists for at least two years, we can consider a cautious taper with appropriate counselling on relapse risk; this will be reviewed jointly at that time. 

I have arranged a routine follow-up in six months, with earlier review if there are any breakthrough events or concerns. 

With best wishes,

Summary/Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy with retained awareness episodes; history of migraine without aura; stage lighting technician with frequent exposure to strobe/bright lights; colleagues may occasionally witness events; mild anxiety symptoms.

Medication: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily. Propranolol 10 mg as required for performance anxiety. Vitamin D 800 IU daily. No known drug allergies.

Thank you for asking me to review this patient in the epilepsy clinic. She works as a stage lighting technician and reports frequent occupational exposure to strobe and very bright moving lights. She and her colleagues have noticed brief spells where she pauses and experiences a rising epigastric sensation with tingling in the right hand and transient difficulty finding words, typically lasting 30–60 seconds without loss of awareness. There is no tongue biting, no incontinence, and recovery is rapid.

Epilepsy classification: The semiology and history are in keeping with focal epilepsy. She has no history suggestive of generalised tonic–clonic seizures. There is no known family history of epilepsy. She does not drink to excess and does not use recreational drugs. Sleep can be irregular during show runs.

Seizures: She presented with dizziness and headaches on consecutive days, but these resolved spontaneously. On 14/May, following the use of her prescribed medication, the absences became less frequent. The last such episode occurred on 20/May and she has been stable since. Given her stability now, I do not propose further intervention for now. For clarity, the events she describes as “absences” correspond to her brief focal aware episodes.

Examination: Neurological examination today was normal. Gait normal. No focal deficits.

Investigations: Previous MRI brain (report available) showed no structural lesion. Routine EEG last year demonstrated intermittent left temporal sharp activity without generalised discharges.

Occupational context: We discussed strobe exposure and she will continue to use agreed workplace measures (avoiding direct strobe view during programming, scheduled breaks, and ensuring adequate sleep). Colleagues are aware of the brief focal episodes and have been advised on simple first aid and observation.

Plan:

- No dose changes at present given current stability.
